bibliographic.citation: Eleanor Prescott Hammond, 'London Lickpenny,' Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 20, neue Folge 8 (1898): 404-420.
bibliographic.title: London Lickpenny

entities.description: The immediate print source: an article with introduction and argument on pages 404-409, parallel manuscript texts and notes on pages 410-419, and a list of later corrections in Harley 367 on page 420.
entities.label: Eleanor Prescott Hammond, London Lickpenny (1898)
notes: Hammond rejects the traditional attribution to John Lydgate: she finds no internal Lydgatian evidence and traces the external attribution to Stow and the unidentified Harley 367 heading.

Hammond considers Harley 367 possibly derived from the Harley 542 recension or from its prototype through omissions and transpositions, but states that the exact relation cannot be confidently determined.

reviewNotes: Hammond's article occupies printed pages 404-420. DIMEV v1.0 gives 404-421, but visual inspection of the complete volume shows that page 420 ends the article and a new article begins on page 421.

The current British Library Harley 542 component description supplies the Harley 367 incipit for this work. Folio range, title, Hammond's edition, and DIMEV securely distinguish the two recensions despite that catalog-level mismatch.
METS describes the Harley 367 copy as executed by John Stow, whereas Hammond describes an unidentified hand contemporary with or slightly later than Stow; the copyist remains unresolved here.

lineage.summary: CME00103 is a repository XML representation of the University of Michigan encoding of the textual portion of Eleanor Prescott Hammond's 1898 article 'London Lickpenny.' The encoding serializes Hammond's parallel transcriptions of two complete recensions: British Library, Harley MS 542, fols. 102r-104r, on the article's even pages 410-418, and Harley MS 367, fols. 127r-v then 126r-v, on odd pages 411-419. It retains three editorial headnote paragraphs and eighteen line notes, but omits Hammond's introduction on pages 404-409 and her after-text list of later Harley 367 corrections on page 420. The two late manuscript copies are distinct witnesses to one anonymous early-fifteenth-century poem; neither is safely modeled as a physical copy of the other.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Early fifteenth century; surviving copies late sixteenth to early seventeenth century
catalogSummary.title: London Lickpenny

catalogingSubject.label: London Lickpenny
catalogingSubject.scope_note: One anonymous early-fifteenth-century poem represented through two complete late manuscript recensions. The recension streams are encoded separately, but they are not cataloged as two independent works.

contentParts.label: Harley MS 542 recension
contentParts.summary: Sixteen eight-line, four-stress ababbcbc stanzas, 128 lines, copied by John Stow. Hammond prints this stream on even pages and treats it as the more archaic and less redacted recension.

contentParts.label: Harley MS 367 recension

locators.value: Harley MS 367, textual order fols. 127r-v then 126r-v

contentParts.summary: Sixteen seven-line, four-stress ababbcc rhyme-royal stanzas, 112 lines. The manuscript's textual order is fols. 127r-v followed by 126r-v; Hammond prints this stream on odd pages.

contentParts.label: Retained Hammond headnotes and line apparatus

locators.value: Three HEADNOTE/P elements and eighteen NOTE elements

notes: Hammond's separate page-420 list of later Harley 367 corrections is not encoded.
contentParts.summary: Three editorial headnote paragraphs and eighteen line-specific notes reporting scribal corrections, readings, and selected comparisons with Skeat. This prose is a modern scholarly layer nested around the verse streams, not a third work item.

assertion.notes: The 1400-1425 range is a cataloging normalization of 'early fifteenth century,' not a precisely documented date.

notes: The surviving witnesses are much later and supply dates of attestation, not composition.

dateStatements.display: Fourth quarter of the sixteenth century (codex cataloged 1575-1599)

assertion.notes: The British Library dates the composite volume through 1624; METS gives c.1600-25 for the copy.

extent.description: Two complete encoded recension items of one work: 16 stanza groups and 128 lines from Harley 542 plus 16 stanza groups and 112 lines from Harley 367. The XML additionally contains three editorial headnote paragraphs and eighteen editorial NOTE elements, excluded from the medieval item counts.

formStatements.description: A four-stress stanzaic poem. Harley 542 has sixteen eight-line ababbcbc stanzas; Harley 367 has sixteen seven-line ababbcc rhyme-royal stanzas.

genres.label: Legal satire
notes: The speaker's failed attempt to obtain legal redress at Westminster is conditioned throughout by demands for money.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin expressions or modern editorial language do not constitute a second work-level language.
London and Westminster are historical/narrative settings; no place of composition is known.
The late witness dates must not be substituted for the poem's early-fifteenth-century composition date.
The three XML P elements and eighteen NOTE elements are Hammond's editorial apparatus, not evidence that the medieval work is mixed prose and verse.
Hammond's article ends on page 420, not page 421; CME00103 encodes only pages 410-419.

next_steps: Collate both manuscripts directly, including Hammond's page-420 later corrections omitted from CME00103.
Review later stemmatic scholarship before asserting that one surviving witness or recension descends from the other.
notes: Hammond allows derivation from the Harley 542 recension or its prototype but says the relation cannot be confidently established.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact textual relationship between the Harley 542 and Harley 367 recensions?

next_steps: Compare high-resolution images or the physical folios with securely identified John Stow and Ralph Starkey hands.
Reconcile Hammond's unidentified contemporary hand with METS's Stow attribution.
openQuestions.question: Who copied the London Lickpenny text in Harley MS 367?

notes: This does not assert a known place of composition.
The poem's speaker begins and ends in Kent; this is narrative setting, not evidence for composition there.

assertion.notes: No securely identified author is known.
The attribution is historically real but not accepted: the Harley 367 heading and Stow assign the poem to Lydgate, while Hammond finds no internal Lydgatian evidence.

scope.description: Modern editorial responsibility for the 1898 representation encoded as CME00103, not medieval authorship.

subjects.label: Urban life and customs
notes: Includes London markets, shops, taverns, street cries, and movement through the city and Westminster.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English estates satire in which a speaker travels from Kent to London and Westminster seeking legal redress. Officials and intermediaries repeatedly demand money; after surveying the city's markets, trades, street cries, food, drink, and sharp dealing, the penniless speaker returns to Kent. The poem survives here in two substantially different sixteen-stanza recensions.

CME00103 serializes Hammond's facing-page edition as two successive version streams: Harley 542, sixteen eight-line stanzas and 128 lines, followed by Harley 367, sixteen rhyme-royal stanzas and 112 lines. Three prose paragraphs are editorial headnotes and eighteen NOTE elements are line-specific apparatus; they are not additional medieval prose works.

The two encoded texts are complete witness recensions of one poem, not two separately authored works. Hammond's 1898 editorial frame is a source-representation layer. The XML includes only the parallel texts and retained apparatus on printed pages 410-419, not the article's introduction on pages 404-409 or its page-420 list of later Harley 367 corrections.

John Lydgate is retained only as a disputed historical attribution. The Harley 367 heading and Stow's Survey associate the poem with Lydgate, but Hammond found no internal evidence for his authorship, and the current METS edition identifies the author as unknown.

notes: Modern preferred spelling; the witness headings vary.
This is the single whole-work discovery incipit. It is not a claim that both witnesses have identical orthography.

scope.description: Normalized discovery incipit used for the shared poem; exact spelling varies by recension.

titles.value: To London once my steps I bent

notes: Heading as represented in CME00103.
The manuscript leaves a blank between 'about' and 'yeres'; the heading's Lydgate claim is historical attribution evidence, not accepted authorship.
titles.value: London Lyckpenny A Ballade compyled by Dan John Lydgate monke of Bery about yeres agoe, and now newly oū'sene and amended.

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Early fifteenth century; surviving copies late sixteenth to early seventeenth century
work.summary: An anonymous Middle English estates satire in which a speaker travels from Kent to London and Westminster seeking legal redress. Officials and intermediaries repeatedly demand money; after surveying the city's markets, trades, street cries, food, drink, and sharp dealing, the penniless speaker returns to Kent. The poem survives here in two substantially different sixteen-stanza recensions.
